title: Au restaurant
description: Ordering, polite expressions, basic menu vocabulary.

cards.notes: **Standard polite order**. Conditional of *vouloir*, but treat it as a fixed phrase at A1. Always more polite than *Je veux* (I want), which sounds blunt to French ears.
Literally *if it pleases you*. Always written with the apostrophe. Often abbreviated **SVP** in text messages or signs.
Adding **beaucoup** intensifies: *merci beaucoup* (thanks a lot). The reply is *de rien* (you're welcome — lit. *of nothing*) or *je vous en prie* (very formal).
The standard way to ask for the check. **L'addition** means *the bill* in restaurants — never *la facture* (which is for invoices / utilities).
Said by staff serving food, AND by table companions before eating. There's no real English equivalent — "enjoy" is closest. Always with the exclamation mark.
On the table by default in most French restaurants — refills are free. *Du pain* (some bread) if you want more.
Already met in Lesson 03. *Une carafe d'eau* (a jug of tap water) is a free, polite request.
*Un verre de vin* (a glass of wine). Watch the **vin** / **vingt** (twenty) pair — same pronunciation, totally different meanings; context decides.
A small espresso by default in France. Ask for *un café allongé* (Americano-style) or *un café au lait* (with milk) if you want something different.
Don't confuse with **le poison** (one s) — that's poison. One letter, big difference.
France produces ~1,000 varieties. *Un plateau de fromages* (a cheese platter) often comes between the main course and dessert.

steps.title: Eating out in France
steps.body:
# Au restaurant

French restaurant culture has its own rhythm — staff greet you, you sit, the *carte* (menu) arrives, and there's no rush. This lesson covers the language pieces you'll need:

- **Polite phrases**: *je voudrais*, *s'il vous plaît*, *merci*
- **Ordering and asking for the bill**
- **Common food vocabulary** for menu reading

A few cultural notes:
- Service is **included** in the price (look for *service compris* on the menu). Tipping is optional; rounding up is appreciated but not required.
- **L'addition** doesn't appear at the table on its own — you have to ask for it.
- **Bread + tap water** are usually free. Ask: *Une carafe d'eau, s'il vous plaît.*

steps.title: Polite ordering
steps.body:
## The polite request pattern

| French | English |
|---|---|
| Je voudrais ___ | I would like ___ |
| ___, s'il vous plaît | ___, please |
| Merci (beaucoup) | Thank you (very much) |
| De rien / Je vous en prie | You're welcome |

**Je voudrais** is the conditional of *vouloir* — at A1 it's a fixed polite formula. Pair it with the article you learned in Lesson 03:

- *Je voudrais **un café**, s'il vous plaît.*
- *Je voudrais **une salade**, s'il vous plaît.*
- *Je voudrais **du pain**, s'il vous plaît.* (some bread — partitive article)

At A1 you don't need to master the partitive (*du / de la / des*) — knowing it exists is enough.

exercise.prompt: Order a coffee politely. Translate: *I would like a coffee, please.*
accept: Je voudrais un café, s'il vous plaît.
Je voudrais un café s'il vous plaît
je voudrais un café, s'il vous plaît
exercise.hint: *Je voudrais* + indefinite article + noun + *s'il vous plaît*.

steps.title: Menu vocabulary
steps.body:
## Food and drink basics

| French | English |
|---|---|
| le pain | bread |
| l'eau | water |
| le vin | wine |
| le café | coffee |
| le poulet | chicken |
| le poisson | fish |
| la salade | salad |
| le fromage | cheese |

Look-alikes to watch:
- **vin** (wine) vs **vingt** (twenty) — same sound, different meaning
- **poisson** (fish) vs **poison** (poison) — one s makes the difference
- **dessert** (dessert) vs **désert** (desert, the dry land) — one s vs two

At A1, you can read most menu items just by recognising these core words plus the adjectives from Lesson 07 (color, but also *grillé* — grilled, *frais* — fresh).

exercise.prompt: Match each French menu word with its English meaning.
Arrange the tiles to ask for a jug of tap water politely.
exercise.hint: *Une carafe d'eau* (a jug of water), then the polite formula.
Definite article (the specific bill for your table), comma, polite formula.

exercise.prompt: The waiter sets your plate down and says…
exercise.hint: Said specifically before someone starts eating.

exercise.prompt: Match each French phrase with when you'd use it.
Arrange to mean: *I would like a glass of wine, please.*
exercise.hint: *Un verre de vin* — a glass of wine. *De* (not *du*) when an indefinite quantity follows *un verre*.
